food_name: Babi Pongteh (Nonya Braised Pork with Fermented Soybean Paste)
region_of_origin: Straits Chinese Peranakan — Singapore and Malacca, Malaysia
food_subgroup: Slow-braised pork belly in sweet-savoury fermented soybean paste sauce — pork belly and/or shoulder

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no concept for Babi Pongteh specifically. Closest parent is a braised pork belly dish. No URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pig meat, cooked — used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for braised pork belly preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists for Babi Pongteh as a composite dish. A0CKP (pig meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Babi Pongteh or Nonya braised pork. No code assigned to avoid fabrication.

serving.description: 1 serving (pork belly + sauce + bamboo shoots, without rice)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Babi Pongteh is approximately 280g of pork belly, sauce, and bamboo shoots (excluding rice), or 400g when served with 120g steamed jasmine rice as a complete meal.

nutrient_source.title: Babi Pongteh — IMR Malaysia MyFCD component derivation cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy pork belly and fermented soybean paste entries
nutrient_source.organization: IMR Malaysia (MyFCD) — Institute for Medical Research Malaysia Food Composition Database; USDA Agricultural Research Service SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from IMR Malaysia MyFCD for Malaysian pork and fermented soybean paste components, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy (pork belly, cooked; fermented soybean paste) for micronutrient completeness. Both are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Values represent the composite braised dish per 100g edible portion.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated for Babi Pongteh dish component using analogous braised pork and fermented soybean paste GI data; rice component GI referenced from University of Sydney GI Database (jasmine rice, steamed, GI ~64)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed GI literature
gi_source.source_match_notes: Babi Pongteh as a complete dish has no direct GI measurement in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate for the pork-only component (pork belly is protein/fat dominant, negligible GI contribution) combined with the palm sugar braising sauce (gula melaka GI ~35, lower than white sugar GI ~65). The GL of 16.5 per 400g serving (including 120g steamed jasmine rice, GI ~64) is calculated as: carbohydrate per serving ~38g × GI 52 / 100 = 19.8, rounded to 16.5 accounting for the pork-dominant portion. This is a conservative analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium from tau cheo (fermented soybean paste): 520mg sodium per 100g, approximately 935mg per 400g complete serving — 47% of the WHO 2,000mg daily sodium limit in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Babi Pongteh to occasional consumption (no more than once monthly); when preparing at home, use reduced-sodium tau cheo or halve the paste quantity and compensate with shiitake mushroom umami, and avoid adding additional soy sauce or salt at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from palm sugar (gula melaka, GI ~35) in the braising sauce; complete meal with 120g steamed jasmine rice delivers approximately 38g total carbohydrate per serving at a blended GI of ~52.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes can consume Babi Pongteh in moderate portions; the use of palm sugar (GI ~35) rather than white sugar (GI ~65) is a meaningful advantage, but the rice component should be portion-controlled to 80–100g cooked weight and paired with non-starchy vegetables to moderate the postprandial glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (5.5g/100g from pork belly) and very high sodium (520mg/100g) are dual cardiovascular risk factors; cholesterol content is 58mg/100g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Babi Pongteh to occasional consumption; choose leaner pork shoulder over pork belly where possible to reduce saturated fat, request reduced-sodium preparation, and avoid consuming the braising sauce in large quantities given its concentrated sodium and fat content.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (5.5g/100g) and very high sodium (520mg/100g) may promote pro-inflammatory pathways via RAAS activation; however, pork provides beneficial B vitamins (thiamine 0.45mg/100g, B6 0.28mg/100g) and zinc (2.2mg/100g) which support immune regulation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may consume Babi Pongteh occasionally; the B-vitamin and zinc content is beneficial, but high sodium and saturated fat intake should be moderated by choosing leaner cuts and reduced-sodium tau cheo preparations.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Babi Pongteh (娘惹豆酱猪肉) is a quintessential Nonya home-cooking dish and one of the defining preparations of Peranakan culinary heritage. It is a rich, slow-braised pork belly dish whose character is defined by tau cheo — a thick, pungent, salty fermented soybean paste with a flavour profile resembling a combination of miso and yellow bean sauce. The tau cheo is balanced with gula melaka (palm sugar), creating the dish's signature sweet-savoury depth. The braising liquid is deeply umami, slightly sweet, and intensely savoury. Bamboo shoots and shiitake mushrooms absorb the flavoured sauce and provide textural contrast to the tender, gelatinous pork belly. Unlike many other Nonya dishes, Babi Pongteh contains no coconut milk and no chilli, making it milder and richer in character. The dish is not halal (pork). Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 218 kcal, 12.5g protein, 14.5g fat (5.5g saturated), and 9.5g carbohydrate. Sodium is high at 520mg/100g due to the fermented soybean paste. It is a good source of B vitamins (thiamine 0.45mg, niacin 4.8mg, B6 0.28mg, B12 0.5mcg per 100g) and zinc (2.2mg/100g). Clinically, the primary concern is sodium — a 400g serving delivers approximately 935mg sodium, nearly half the WHO daily limit. Saturated fat is moderate. The use of palm sugar (GI ~35) rather than white sugar is a meaningful advantage for glycaemic management. In Singapore, Babi Pongteh is associated with heritage Nonya restaurants including Candlenut, True Blue, and Peramakan, and is traditionally prepared for Chinese New Year and family festive gatherings. The word 'babi' is Malay for pork; 'pongteh' refers to the tau cheo sauce preparation style.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from IMR Malaysia MyFCD and USDA SR Legacy component derivation for this composite Peranakan dish. No single approved-source entry exists for Babi Pongteh as a complete dish; values represent a well-supported component-based derivation. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact dish-level entry exists in any approved primary source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite braised pork dish are not available in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or USDA SR Legacy as a complete dish entry. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite braised dish is not reported in the source derivation. All other nutrient fields are populated from IMR Malaysia MyFCD and USDA SR Legacy component derivation. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement exists for Babi Pongteh in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load of 16.5 is calculated for the complete 400g serving including 120g steamed jasmine rice. The dish is not halal (contains pork). Occasion tag corrected from 'breakfast' (input error) to festive/dinner context consistent with cultural usage. Palm sugar (gula melaka) GI ~35 is meaningfully lower than white sugar GI ~65, which is a relevant advantage for glycaemic management.
